narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, LSAT score of 35.50, upper-middle family income, full-time student status, and school deciles from early years (3rd decile in Year 1 and 2nd in Year 3)—and predicted a 57.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This probability crossed the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the lower part of your class (well below the typical 5.5 average), signaling to the model a potential struggle with the rigorous demands ahead. Your LSAT score of 35.50, slightly under the dataset average of 36.70, was the next biggest influence but worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward a higher chance of passing by showing solid standardized reasoning skills. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50 exceeded the average of 3.21, providing another positive boost that suggested strong prior academic capability. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early-year deciles align more with those who face challenges, while your GPA and LSAT are competitive but couldn't fully offset the Year 3 dip. While your upper-middle income and full-time status are common and neutral here, they didn't sway the outcome. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student—many in similar demographics succeed with targeted improvements— but a data-driven call based on patterns in bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push closer to or above 37, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your upward trajectory. Schools value resilience, and addressing that key Year 3 weakness could flip the model's prediction in your favor.
